lines: Yes, and shall do till the pangs of death
shake him. Infirmity, that decays the wise, doth
ever make the better Fool.
fullText: Yes, and shall do till the pangs of death shake him. Infirmity, that decays the wise, doth ever make the better Fool.

lines: I marvel your Ladyship takes delight in
such a barren rascal. I saw him put down the other
day with an ordinary fool that has no more brain
than a stone. Look you now, he's out of his guard
already. Unless you laugh and minister occasion to
him, he is gagged. I protest I take these wise men
that crow so at these set kind of Fools no better than
the Fools' zanies.
fullText: I marvel your Ladyship takes delight in such a barren rascal. I saw him put down the other day with an ordinary fool that has no more brain than a stone. Look you now, he's out of his guard already. Unless you laugh and minister occasion to him, he is gagged. I protest I take these wise men that crow so at these set kind of Fools no better than the Fools' zanies.

lines: Madam, yond young fellow swears he will
speak with you. I told him you were sick; he takes
on him to understand so much, and therefore
comes to speak with you. I told him you were
asleep; he seems to have a foreknowledge of that
too, and therefore comes to speak with you. What is
to be said to him, lady? He's fortified against any
denial.
fullText: Madam, yond young fellow swears he will speak with you. I told him you were sick; he takes on him to understand so much, and therefore comes to speak with you. I told him you were asleep; he seems to have a foreknowledge of that too, and therefore comes to speak with you. What is to be said to him, lady? He's fortified against any denial.

lines: Has been told so, and he says he'll stand at
your door like a sheriff's post and be the supporter
to a bench, but he'll speak with you.
fullText: Has been told so, and he says he'll stand at your door like a sheriff's post and be the supporter to a bench, but he'll speak with you.

lines: Why, of mankind.
fullText: Why, of mankind.

lines: Of very ill manner. He'll speak with you,
will you or no.
fullText: Of very ill manner. He'll speak with you, will you or no.

lines: Not yet old enough for a man, nor young
enough for a boy--as a squash is before 'tis a
peascod, or a codling when 'tis almost an apple. 'Tis
with him in standing water, between boy and man.
He is very well-favored, and he speaks very shrewishly.
One would think his mother's milk were
scarce out of him.
fullText: Not yet old enough for a man, nor young enough for a boy--as a squash is before 'tis a peascod, or a codling when 'tis almost an apple. 'Tis with him in standing water, between boy and man. He is very well-favored, and he speaks very shrewishly. One would think his mother's milk were scarce out of him.

lines: Gentlewoman, my lady calls.
fullText: Gentlewoman, my lady calls.

lines: Here, madam, at your service.
fullText: Here, madam, at your service.

lines: Madam, I will.
fullText: Madam, I will.

lines: Were not you even now with the Countess
Olivia?
fullText: Were not you even now with the Countess Olivia?

lines: She returns this ring to you, sir. You might
have saved me my pains to have taken it away
yourself. She adds, moreover, that you should put
your lord into a desperate assurance she will none
of him. And one thing more, that you be never so
hardy to come again in his affairs unless it be to
report your lord's taking of this. Receive it so.
fullText: She returns this ring to you, sir. You might have saved me my pains to have taken it away yourself. She adds, moreover, that you should put your lord into a desperate assurance she will none of him. And one thing more, that you be never so hardy to come again in his affairs unless it be to report your lord's taking of this. Receive it so.

lines: Come, sir, you peevishly threw it to her, and
her will is it should be so returned. [He throws
down the ring.] If it be worth stooping for, there it
lies in your eye; if not, be it his that finds it.
fullText: Come, sir, you peevishly threw it to her, and her will is it should be so returned. [He throws down the ring.] If it be worth stooping for, there it lies in your eye; if not, be it his that finds it.

lines: My masters, are you mad? Or what are you?
Have you no wit, manners, nor honesty but to
gabble like tinkers at this time of night? Do you
make an ale-house of my lady's house, that you
squeak out your coziers' catches without any mitigation
or remorse of voice? Is there no respect of
place, persons, nor time in you?
fullText: My masters, are you mad? Or what are you? Have you no wit, manners, nor honesty but to gabble like tinkers at this time of night? Do you make an ale-house of my lady's house, that you squeak out your coziers' catches without any mitigation or remorse of voice? Is there no respect of place, persons, nor time in you?

lines: Sir Toby, I must be round with you. My lady
bade me tell you that, though she harbors you as her
kinsman, she's nothing allied to your disorders. If
you can separate yourself and your misdemeanors,
you are welcome to the house; if not, an it would
please you to take leave of her, she is very willing to
bid you farewell.
fullText: Sir Toby, I must be round with you. My lady bade me tell you that, though she harbors you as her kinsman, she's nothing allied to your disorders. If you can separate yourself and your misdemeanors, you are welcome to the house; if not, an it would please you to take leave of her, she is very willing to bid you farewell.

lines: Is 't even so?
fullText: Is 't even so?

lines: This is much credit to you.
fullText: This is much credit to you.

lines: Mistress Mary, if you prized my lady's favor
at anything more than contempt, you would not give
means for this uncivil rule. She shall know of it, by
this hand.
fullText: Mistress Mary, if you prized my lady's favor at anything more than contempt, you would not give means for this uncivil rule. She shall know of it, by this hand.

lines: 'Tis but fortune, all is fortune. Maria once
told me she did affect me, and I have heard herself
come thus near, that should she fancy, it should be
one of my complexion. Besides, she uses me with a
more exalted respect than anyone else that follows
her. What should I think on 't?
fullText: 'Tis but fortune, all is fortune. Maria once told me she did affect me, and I have heard herself come thus near, that should she fancy, it should be one of my complexion. Besides, she uses me with a more exalted respect than anyone else that follows her. What should I think on 't?

lines: To be Count Malvolio.
fullText: To be Count Malvolio.

lines: There is example for 't. The lady of the
Strachy married the yeoman of the wardrobe.
fullText: There is example for 't. The lady of the Strachy married the yeoman of the wardrobe.

lines: Having been three months married to her,
sitting in my state--
fullText: Having been three months married to her, sitting in my state--

lines: Calling my officers about me, in my
branched velvet gown, having come from a daybed
where I have left Olivia sleeping--
fullText: Calling my officers about me, in my branched velvet gown, having come from a daybed where I have left Olivia sleeping--

lines: And then to have the humor of state; and
after a demure travel of regard, telling them I
know my place, as I would they should do theirs, to
ask for my kinsman Toby--
fullText: And then to have the humor of state; and after a demure travel of regard, telling them I know my place, as I would they should do theirs, to ask for my kinsman Toby--

lines: Seven of my people, with an obedient start,
make out for him. I frown the while, and perchance
wind up my watch, or play with my--some
rich jewel. Toby approaches; curtsies there to me--
fullText: Seven of my people, with an obedient start, make out for him. I frown the while, and perchance wind up my watch, or play with my--some rich jewel. Toby approaches; curtsies there to me--

lines: I extend my hand to him thus, quenching
my familiar smile with an austere regard of
control--
fullText: I extend my hand to him thus, quenching my familiar smile with an austere regard of control--

lines: Saying, "Cousin Toby, my fortunes, having
cast me on your niece, give me this prerogative of
speech--"
fullText: Saying, "Cousin Toby, my fortunes, having cast me on your niece, give me this prerogative of speech--"

lines: "You must amend your drunkenness."
fullText: "You must amend your drunkenness."

lines: "Besides, you waste the treasure of your
time with a foolish knight--"
fullText: "Besides, you waste the treasure of your time with a foolish knight--"

lines: "One Sir Andrew."
fullText: "One Sir Andrew."

lines: What employment have
we here?
fullText: What employment have we here?

lines: By my life, this is my
lady's hand! These be her very c's, her u's, and her
t's, and thus she makes her great P's. It is in
contempt of question her hand.
fullText: By my life, this is my lady's hand! These be her very c's, her u's, and her t's, and thus she makes her great P's. It is in contempt of question her hand.

lines: "M.O.A.I. doth sway my life." Nay, but first
let me see, let me see, let me see.
fullText: "M.O.A.I. doth sway my life." Nay, but first let me see, let me see, let me see.

lines: "I may command where I adore." Why, she
may command me; I serve her; she is my lady. Why,
this is evident to any formal capacity. There is no
obstruction in this. And the end--what should that
alphabetical position portend? If I could make that
resemble something in me! Softly! "M.O.A.I."--
fullText: "I may command where I adore." Why, she may command me; I serve her; she is my lady. Why, this is evident to any formal capacity. There is no obstruction in this. And the end--what should that alphabetical position portend? If I could make that resemble something in me! Softly! "M.O.A.I."--

lines: "M"--Malvolio. "M"--why, that begins
my name!
fullText: "M"--Malvolio. "M"--why, that begins my name!

lines: "M." But then there is no consonancy in
the sequel that suffers under probation. "A" should
follow, but "O" does.
fullText: "M." But then there is no consonancy in the sequel that suffers under probation. "A" should follow, but "O" does.

lines: And then "I" comes behind.
fullText: And then "I" comes behind.

lines: "M.O.A.I." This simulation is not as the
former, and yet to crush this a little, it would bow
to me, for every one of these letters are in my name.
Soft, here follows prose.
stars I am above thee, but be not afraid of greatness.
Some are born great, some achieve greatness, and
some have greatness thrust upon 'em. Thy fates open
their hands. Let thy blood and spirit embrace them.
And, to inure thyself to what thou art like to be, cast
thy humble slough and appear fresh. Be opposite with
a kinsman, surly with servants. Let thy tongue tang
arguments of state. Put thyself into the trick of singularity.
She thus advises thee that sighs for thee.
Remember who commended thy yellow stockings and
wished to see thee ever cross-gartered. I say, remember.
Go to, thou art made, if thou desir'st to be so. If
not, let me see thee a steward still, the fellow of
servants, and not worthy to touch Fortune's fingers.
Farewell. She that would alter services with thee,
The Fortunate-Unhappy.
Daylight and champian discovers not more! This is
open. I will be proud, I will read politic authors, I
will baffle Sir Toby, I will wash off gross acquaintance,
I will be point-devise the very man. I do not
now fool myself, to let imagination jade me; for
every reason excites to this, that my lady loves me.
She did commend my yellow stockings of late, she
did praise my leg being cross-gartered, and in this
she manifests herself to my love and, with a kind of
injunction, drives me to these habits of her liking. I
thank my stars, I am happy. I will be strange, stout,
in yellow stockings, and cross-gartered, even with
the swiftness of putting on. Jove and my stars be
praised! Here is yet a postscript.
am. If thou entertain'st my love, let it appear in thy
smiling; thy smiles become thee well. Therefore in my
presence still smile, dear my sweet, I prithee.
Jove, I thank thee! I will smile. I will do everything
that thou wilt have me.
fullText: "M.O.A.I." This simulation is not as the former, and yet to crush this a little, it would bow to me, for every one of these letters are in my name. Soft, here follows prose. stars I am above thee, but be not afraid of greatness. Some are born great, some achieve greatness, and some have greatness thrust upon 'em. Thy fates open their hands. Let thy blood and spirit embrace them. And, to inure thyself to what thou art like to be, cast thy humble slough and appear fresh. Be opposite with a kinsman, surly with servants. Let thy tongue tang arguments of state. Put thyself into the trick of singularity. She thus advises thee that sighs for thee. Remember who commended thy yellow stockings and wished to see thee ever cross-gartered. I say, remember. Go to, thou art made, if thou desir'st to be so. If not, let me see thee a steward still, the fellow of servants, and not worthy to touch Fortune's fingers. Farewell. She that would alter services with thee, The Fortunate-Unhappy. Daylight and champian discovers not more! This is open. I will be proud, I will read politic authors, I will baffle Sir Toby, I will wash off gross acquaintance, I will be point-devise the very man. I do not now fool myself, to let imagination jade me; for every reason excites to this, that my lady loves me. She did commend my yellow stockings of late, she did praise my leg being cross-gartered, and in this she manifests herself to my love and, with a kind of injunction, drives me to these habits of her liking. I thank my stars, I am happy. I will be strange, stout, in yellow stockings, and cross-gartered, even with the swiftness of putting on. Jove and my stars be praised! Here is yet a postscript. am. If thou entertain'st my love, let it appear in thy smiling; thy smiles become thee well. Therefore in my presence still smile, dear my sweet, I prithee. Jove, I thank thee! I will smile. I will do everything that thou wilt have me.

lines: Sweet lady, ho, ho!
fullText: Sweet lady, ho, ho!

lines: Sad, lady? I could be sad. This does make
some obstruction in the blood, this cross-gartering,
but what of that? If it please the eye of one, it is
with me as the very true sonnet is: "Please one, and
please all."
fullText: Sad, lady? I could be sad. This does make some obstruction in the blood, this cross-gartering, but what of that? If it please the eye of one, it is with me as the very true sonnet is: "Please one, and please all."

lines: Not black in my mind, though yellow in my
legs. It did come to his hands, and commands shall
be executed. I think we do know the sweet Roman
hand.
fullText: Not black in my mind, though yellow in my legs. It did come to his hands, and commands shall be executed. I think we do know the sweet Roman hand.

lines: To bed? "Ay, sweetheart, and I'll come to
thee."
fullText: To bed? "Ay, sweetheart, and I'll come to thee."

lines: At your request? Yes, nightingales answer
daws!
fullText: At your request? Yes, nightingales answer daws!

lines: "Be not afraid of greatness." 'Twas well
writ.
fullText: "Be not afraid of greatness." 'Twas well writ.

lines: "Some are born great--"
fullText: "Some are born great--"

lines: "Some achieve greatness--"
fullText: "Some achieve greatness--"

lines: "And some have greatness thrust upon
them."
fullText: "And some have greatness thrust upon them."

lines: "Remember who commended thy yellow
stockings--"
fullText: "Remember who commended thy yellow stockings--"

lines: "And wished to see thee cross-gartered."
fullText: "And wished to see thee cross-gartered."

lines: "Go to, thou art made, if thou desir'st to be
so--"
fullText: "Go to, thou art made, if thou desir'st to be so--"

lines: "If not, let me see thee a servant still."
fullText: "If not, let me see thee a servant still."

lines: O ho, do you come near me now? No worse
man than Sir Toby to look to me. This concurs
directly with the letter. She sends him on purpose
that I may appear stubborn to him, for she incites
me to that in the letter: "Cast thy humble slough,"
says she. "Be opposite with a kinsman, surly with
servants; let thy tongue tang with arguments of
state; put thyself into the trick of singularity," and
consequently sets down the manner how: as, a sad
face, a reverend carriage, a slow tongue, in the habit
of some Sir of note, and so forth. I have limed her,
but it is Jove's doing, and Jove make me thankful!
And when she went away now, "Let this fellow be
looked to." "Fellow!" Not "Malvolio," nor after my
degree, but "fellow." Why, everything adheres together,
that no dram of a scruple, no scruple of a
scruple, no obstacle, no incredulous or unsafe
circumstance--what can be said? Nothing that can
be can come between me and the full prospect of
my hopes. Well, Jove, not I, is the doer of this, and
he is to be thanked.
fullText: O ho, do you come near me now? No worse man than Sir Toby to look to me. This concurs directly with the letter. She sends him on purpose that I may appear stubborn to him, for she incites me to that in the letter: "Cast thy humble slough," says she. "Be opposite with a kinsman, surly with servants; let thy tongue tang with arguments of state; put thyself into the trick of singularity," and consequently sets down the manner how: as, a sad face, a reverend carriage, a slow tongue, in the habit of some Sir of note, and so forth. I have limed her, but it is Jove's doing, and Jove make me thankful! And when she went away now, "Let this fellow be looked to." "Fellow!" Not "Malvolio," nor after my degree, but "fellow." Why, everything adheres together, that no dram of a scruple, no scruple of a scruple, no obstacle, no incredulous or unsafe circumstance--what can be said? Nothing that can be can come between me and the full prospect of my hopes. Well, Jove, not I, is the doer of this, and he is to be thanked.

lines: Go off, I discard you. Let me enjoy my
private. Go off.
fullText: Go off, I discard you. Let me enjoy my private. Go off.

lines: Aha, does she so?
fullText: Aha, does she so?

lines: Do you know what you say?
fullText: Do you know what you say?

lines: How now, mistress?
fullText: How now, mistress?

lines: Sir!
fullText: Sir!

lines: My prayers, minx?
fullText: My prayers, minx?

lines: Go hang yourselves all! You are idle, shallow
things. I am not of your element. You shall
know more hereafter.
fullText: Go hang yourselves all! You are idle, shallow things. I am not of your element. You shall know more hereafter.

lines: Who calls there?
fullText: Who calls there?

lines: Sir Topas, Sir Topas, good Sir Topas, go to
my lady--
fullText: Sir Topas, Sir Topas, good Sir Topas, go to my lady--

lines: Sir Topas, never was man thus wronged.
Good Sir Topas, do not think I am mad. They have
laid me here in hideous darkness--
fullText: Sir Topas, never was man thus wronged. Good Sir Topas, do not think I am mad. They have laid me here in hideous darkness--

lines: As hell, Sir Topas.
fullText: As hell, Sir Topas.

lines: I am not mad, Sir Topas. I say to you this
house is dark.
fullText: I am not mad, Sir Topas. I say to you this house is dark.

lines: I say this house is as dark as ignorance,
though ignorance were as dark as hell. And I say
there was never man thus abused. I am no more
mad than you are. Make the trial of it in any
constant question.
fullText: I say this house is as dark as ignorance, though ignorance were as dark as hell. And I say there was never man thus abused. I am no more mad than you are. Make the trial of it in any constant question.

lines: That the soul of our grandam might haply
inhabit a bird.
fullText: That the soul of our grandam might haply inhabit a bird.

lines: I think nobly of the soul, and no way
approve his opinion.
fullText: I think nobly of the soul, and no way approve his opinion.

lines: Sir Topas, Sir Topas!
fullText: Sir Topas, Sir Topas!

lines: Fool!
fullText: Fool!

lines: Fool!
fullText: Fool!

lines: Fool, I say!
fullText: Fool, I say!

lines: Good fool, as ever thou wilt deserve well at
my hand, help me to a candle, and pen, ink, and
paper. As I am a gentleman, I will live to be thankful
to thee for 't.
fullText: Good fool, as ever thou wilt deserve well at my hand, help me to a candle, and pen, ink, and paper. As I am a gentleman, I will live to be thankful to thee for 't.

lines: Ay, good Fool.
fullText: Ay, good Fool.

lines: Fool, there was never man so notoriously
abused. I am as well in my wits, Fool, as thou art.
fullText: Fool, there was never man so notoriously abused. I am as well in my wits, Fool, as thou art.

lines: They have here propertied me, keep me in
darkness, send ministers to me--asses!--and do
all they can to face me out of my wits.
fullText: They have here propertied me, keep me in darkness, send ministers to me--asses!--and do all they can to face me out of my wits.

lines: Sir Topas!
fullText: Sir Topas!

lines: Fool! Fool! Fool, I say!
fullText: Fool! Fool! Fool, I say!

lines: Good Fool, help me to some light and some
paper. I tell thee, I am as well in my wits as any
man in Illyria.
fullText: Good Fool, help me to some light and some paper. I tell thee, I am as well in my wits as any man in Illyria.

lines: By this hand, I am. Good Fool, some ink,
paper, and light; and convey what I will set down to
my lady. It shall advantage thee more than ever the
bearing of letter did.
fullText: By this hand, I am. Good Fool, some ink, paper, and light; and convey what I will set down to my lady. It shall advantage thee more than ever the bearing of letter did.

lines: Believe me, I am not. I tell thee true.
fullText: Believe me, I am not. I tell thee true.

lines: Fool, I'll requite it in the highest degree. I
prithee, begone.
fullText: Fool, I'll requite it in the highest degree. I prithee, begone.

lines: Madam, you have done me
wrong,
Notorious wrong.
fullText: Madam, you have done me wrong, Notorious wrong.

lines: Lady, you have. Pray you peruse that letter.
You must not now deny it is your hand.
Write from it if you can, in hand or phrase,
Or say 'tis not your seal, not your invention.
You can say none of this. Well, grant it then,
And tell me, in the modesty of honor,
Why you have given me such clear lights of favor?
Bade me come smiling and cross-gartered to you,
To put on yellow stockings, and to frown
Upon Sir Toby and the lighter people?
And, acting this in an obedient hope,
Why have you suffered me to be imprisoned,
Kept in a dark house, visited by the priest,
And made the most notorious geck and gull
That e'er invention played on? Tell me why.
fullText: Lady, you have. Pray you peruse that letter. You must not now deny it is your hand. Write from it if you can, in hand or phrase, Or say 'tis not your seal, not your invention. You can say none of this. Well, grant it then, And tell me, in the modesty of honor, Why you have given me such clear lights of favor? Bade me come smiling and cross-gartered to you, To put on yellow stockings, and to frown Upon Sir Toby and the lighter people? And, acting this in an obedient hope, Why have you suffered me to be imprisoned, Kept in a dark house, visited by the priest, And made the most notorious geck and gull That e'er invention played on? Tell me why.

lines: I'll be revenged on the whole pack of you!
fullText: I'll be revenged on the whole pack of you!
